misconceptionSummary: Students use the diagonal of a parallelogram as a side length in the perimeter.

lines.text: Parallelogram: base 10 cm, slant side 6 cm, height 5 cm
Used perpendicular height instead of slant side

steps.narration: The student used the perpendicular height — five centimetres — as a side length. But the height is not a side. The sides of the parallelogram are the base and the slant edge. The height is only used for area.

visuals.ruleText: Perimeter uses the actual side lengths — never the perpendicular height
visuals.subText: The slant side is longer than the height — it is the actual boundary

steps.narration: The perpendicular height drops at right angles inside the parallelogram — it is not part of the boundary. The slant side is the actual edge you would walk along. Use the slant side for perimeter.

lines.text: Sides: base 10 cm, slant side 6 cm

steps.narration: Use the base and the slant side: two times ten plus six is thirty-two centimetres. The height of five centimetres plays no part in the perimeter.

visuals.label: Use slant sides — perpendicular height is for area only

steps.narration: Thirty-two centimetres. For perimeter, always use the actual side lengths — the slant sides you would walk along, not the internal height.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: using the perpendicular height as a side in P = 2(b + h). The height is for area. Perimeter uses the actual slant side — the boundary edge.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The perpendicular height is inside the shape — it is not a boundary edge. Always use the slant side for perimeter. Height is for area calculations only.
